file_name: Waqar et al. - 2025 - A comparative study of convolutional neural networks (cnn) and inception v3 for facial skin disease.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Inception V3, to classify five facial skin diseases (acne, actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, eczema, and rosacea) using 1,250 DermNet images. The study compared their performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 scores.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work comparing Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Inception V3 models for classifying five facial skin diseases using a dataset of 1,250 DermNet images. It includes specific methodology (data preprocessing, model architecture, training parameters), results (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 scores), and conclusions derived from this new analysis, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A COMPARATIVE STUDY OF CONVOLUTIONAL NEURAL NETWORKS (CNN) AND INCEPTION V3 FOR FACIAL SKIN DISEASE CLASSIFICATION
brief_summary: This study compared Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Inception V3 for classifying five facial skin diseases: acne, actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, eczema, and rosacea. The researchers utilized a dataset of 1,250 images extracted from DermNet, splitting them into training, validation, and testing sets. Both models employed Adam optimization and categorical cross-entropy loss, with Inception V3 incorporating transfer learning and dense layers while the custom CNN used five convolutional and two dense layers. Inception V3 achieved a test accuracy of 94%, outperforming the CNN's 93%, and demonstrated strong macro-average precision and recall of 0.83. The investigation concluded that Inception V3 exhibits robust capabilities for diagnosing facial skin diseases, though future work should involve larger datasets and clinical testing.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the five facial skin diseases studied in the classification experiment. 250 images were used from the DermNet dataset. The Inception V3 model achieved an F1 score of 0.86 for this class.

all_task_types.target: Five facial skin disease categories: acne, actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, eczema, and rosacea.
all_task_types.details: The study performs multiclass classification of facial skin diseases using CNN and Inception V3 models. The models predict discrete diagnostic labels for five specific conditions. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 scores, with categorical cross-entropy loss used during training.

datasets_used.name: Facial Skin Diseases Dataset (derived from DermNet)
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset created by the authors by extracting images from the DermNet Skin Disorder Atlas, a public database containing over 23,000 clinically described skin disorders. The dataset was filtered to include only five specific facial skin disease categories.

use.training: Used for training the CNN and Inception V3 models (80% of data).
use.validation: Used for validation during model training (10% of data).
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation of the models (10% of data).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was compared using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 scores.

Facial Skin Diseases Dataset (DermNet).notes: Inception V3 achieved a test accuracy of 94%, outperforming the CNN's 93%. Inception V3 showed strong generalization with a macro-average precision and recall of 0.83. The CNN model demonstrated signs of overfitting, with training accuracy reaching 88% but test accuracy only 80% (or 93% in the final comparison table, though text notes 80% testing accuracy in section 4.3). CNN proved slightly better at identifying Eczema with an F1 score of 0.81 compared to Inception V3's 0.79. Inception V3 achieved an F1 score of 0.86 for Acne and 0.84 for Rosacea.

architecture_used.main_model: Inception V3 (Transfer Learning) and Custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)
architecture_used.pretraining: Inception V3 was pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset (1000 categories). The pre-trained weights were loaded from Keras, and the feature extraction layers were frozen (trainable=False). The custom CNN was trained from scratch.
architecture_used.input_size: 244x244 pixels (resized from original images). Inception V3 natively supports 299x299, but the study resized inputs to 244x244.

Inception V3.added_layers: 5 additional layers: Flatten, Dense (256 units, ReLU), Batch Normalization, Dropout (20%), Dense (5 units, SoftMax)

classification_or_output_head.Inception V3: Dense layer (256 units, ReLU) -> Batch Normalization -> Dropout (20%) -> Dense layer (5 units, SoftMax)
classification_or_output_head.Custom CNN: Dense layer (128 units, ReLU) -> Dense layer (5 units, SoftMax)

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to 224x244 pixels (Section 3.2.1) and 244x244 pixels (Section 3.3.4)
Pixel value normalization scaling RGB values from 0-255 to the 0-1 range using Keras ImageDataGenerator (Section 3.2.1)
Data augmentation to boost dataset quantity and model generalization (Section 3.2.2)
Quality control filtering to eliminate raw or low-quality images (Section 3.1.3)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied shearing transformation to slant the image content, adding variability.
Introduced zoom in/out transformations to modify the image scale.
Enabled horizontal flipping to introduce mirrored versions of images.
Images are normalized by dividing pixel values by 255 (scaling RGB values from 0-255 to 0-1 range).
The ImageDataGenerator object produces batches containing 32 transformed images during training.

conclusions: Inception V3 outperformed the custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) in classifying five facial skin diseases (acne, actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, eczema, and rosacea), achieving a test accuracy of 94% compared to the CNN's 93%.

The Inception V3 model demonstrated superior generalization capabilities, maintaining stable validation scores (93-94%) and high macro-average precision and recall (0.83), whereas the CNN model showed signs of overfitting with a training accuracy of 88% but a lower testing accuracy of 80%.
Inception V3 achieved higher F1 scores for acne (0.86) and rosacea (0.84) compared to the CNN, while the CNN model proved slightly better at identifying eczema with an F1 score of 0.81.

The study concludes that Inception V3 exhibits strong capabilities for diagnosing facial skin diseases through accurate and scalable results, leveraging transfer learning to maintain performance despite a restricted dataset size of 1,250 images.

The authors acknowledge limitations regarding the small dataset size and recommend future research to increase the dataset size and conduct clinical tests to enhance universal usage and real-world applicability.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, custom dataset of only 1,250 images extracted from DermNet, which limits the generalizability and robustness of the findings.

The dataset consists of equal class distribution (250 images per class), which does not reflect the real-world prevalence of these skin diseases and may mask performance issues on imbalanced clinical data.

There is a discrepancy in the reported testing accuracy for the CNN model: the abstract and results section state 93%, while the detailed analysis in Section 4.3 and Figure 15 report a testing accuracy of 80%, suggesting potential reporting inconsistency or overfitting.
The CNN model showed signs of overfitting, with training accuracy reaching 88% but lower validation (84%) and testing (80%) accuracy, whereas Inception V3 demonstrated better generalization.

The study lacks external validation; the models were only tested on the held-out portion of the DermNet-derived dataset, raising concerns about performance on diverse clinical populations or different image acquisition devices.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to determine if the performance difference between Inception V3 (94%) and CNN (93%) is statistically significant.
The dataset was curated by filtering DermNet, which may introduce selection bias as the images are not necessarily representative of the full spectrum of clinical presentations for these conditions.
The authors acknowledge the need for larger datasets and clinical testing to enhance universal usage, indicating the current evidence is preliminary and not yet ready for clinical deployment.
